coredata.dc:description: With the need of renewable energy sources electrocatalytic water splitting has been developed greatly. Meanwhile, it is very crucial to choose a suitable electrocatalyst for designing advanced catalytic systems to fulfill the requirements of the modern world. Two dimensional (2D) materials (graphene/MXene) and their modified phases have displayed excellent species as electrocatalysts. In this report we synthesized 2D Ta4C3Tx MXene and zero-dimensional (0D) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) to further prepare GQD/Ta4C3Tx MXene nano-heterostructures using cost effective and easy approaches. The supporting characterizations helped in evaluating the good quality synthesis of nano-heterostructure. All these 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene heterostructures demonstrated enhanced OER and HER catalytic activities. With varying distribution of GQD (1.5%–10%) over Ta4C3Tx sheets, 5%GQD/MXene has been found to be the most efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting. Moreover, GQD addition enhances the active sites as well as the faster reaction kinetics to achieve a low charge transfer resistance of 80 Ω, an onset potential of 1.5 V as well as performance stability up to 97% for extended time periods. Hence, this study offers insight for tuning MXene phases with dense structure and large flakes using an effective approach in order to overcome the challenges associated with advanced water splitting applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the need of renewable energy sources electrocatalytic water splitting has been developed greatly. Meanwhile, it is very crucial to choose a suitable electrocatalyst for designing advanced catalytic systems to fulfill the requirements of the modern world. Two dimensional (2D) materials (graphene/MXene) and their modified phases have displayed excellent species as electrocatalysts. In this report we synthesized 2D Ta4C3Tx MXene and zero-dimensional (0D) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) to further prepare GQD/Ta4C3Tx MXene nano-heterostructures using cost effective and easy approaches. The supporting characterizations helped in evaluating the good quality synthesis of nano-heterostructure. All these 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene heterostructures demonstrated enhanced OER and HER catalytic activities. With varying distribution of GQD (1.5%–10%) over Ta4C3Tx sheets, 5%GQD/MXene has been found to be the most efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting. Moreover, GQD addition enhances the active sites as well as the faster reaction kinetics to achieve a low charge transfer resistance of 80 Ω, an onset potential of 1.5 V as well as performance stability up to 97% for extended time periods. Hence, this study offers insight for tuning MXene phases with dense structure and large flakes using an effective approach in order to overcome the challenges associated with advanced water splitting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, global warming acceleration has caused several environmental issues and energy crises due to which there is an ever growing need to produce renewable clean energy and conversion [1]. Researchers are using other different energy sources like wind, solar and geothermal to fulfill the needs but the utilization of these sources is limited due to their periodic and erratic characteristics [2]. Hence, to ensure the required demand, energy storage is also required. Among all available storage systems, hydrogen (H2) storage has garnered a significant interest of the community for providing substantial ecological benefits and sufficient energy density [3]. Hydrogen production via fuel burning also causes environmental pollution due to the release of carbon dioxide CO2 in the process. So, for clean H2 production water electrolysis is considered as a sustainable and innovative way via renewable sources without emitting CO2. But its commercialization is challenging because of significant energy barriers which prohibit its proper functioning and must be removed [4]. In water splitting, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) as well as oxygen (O2) evolution reaction (OER) are the key processes to produce H2 and O2 within an electrochemical cell. Therefore, there is a need to design electrocatalysts having low activation energies for HER and OER process to promote suitable kinetics for industrial applications [5,6]. Nobel metal/metal oxide (Ir, Ru, RuO2 etc.) have been proved efficient electrocatalysts for water splitting. Also, under specific conditions most of the Nobel metal catalysts function best only for HER or OER [7,8]. Still the exceeded cost and limited availability significantly hindered their commercialization. Therefore, bifunctional catalysts that are cost effective and can perform both anodic as well as cathodic reactions were developed to further simplify the devices and perform more efficient water catalysis [9–11].

Previously several material systems like perovskites [12–14], transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [15], carbides [16], phosphides [17], metal organic frameworks (MOF) [18] and polymers [19] etc. have been studied and modified for electrocatalysis. Among all these efforts lately a class of two dimensional (2D) materials have been proved to performing as exceptional applicants in all aspects of scientific fields such as energy storage [20,21], data storage [22,23], photocatalysis [24,25], ferroelectrics [26,27], and water splitting [28]. Graphene is a candidate of 2D materials family consisted of single hexagonal sheet of carbon atoms and is widely studied for its exceptional performance and properties [29–31]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are the zero dimensional (0D) derivatives of the graphene and are also extensively explored throughout all research areas such as sensors, electrocatalysts, supercapacitors etc. [32–35] Due to their small dimensionality (approximately less than 10 nm) and outstanding edge effects GQD have been the main attraction of the applied and material science fields [36,37]. They have been explored in the field of water splitting due to the benefits of providing more active sites and abundant defects [38,39]. Graphene quantum dots with nitrogen doping have been proved to provide increased reaction cites and easy charge transfer to increase the catalytic efficiencies [40,41]. But they have an ability to cluster/aggregate which is why it is important to combine or modify them with 2D layered materials for enhancing the overall performances [42].

MXene also belongs to a class of layered 2D materials including single metal transition metal carbides/nitrides (Ti3C2, Ti2N etc.) and double transition metal MXene (Mo2TiC2, Cr2TiC2, etc.) with a general class formula as Mn+1XnTx. This originates from the MAX where M is metal (single or double transition), A is either aluminum (Al) or silicon (Si) and X belongs to carbon (C) or nitrogen (N) [43]. Numerous methods have been used till now to remove the Al layer from the 3D MAX phase to get etched MXene phase. Moreover, certain polymers such as tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (TBAOH) and tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) are also employed to exfoliate the etched MXene sheets from each other to obtain 2D MXene Mn+1XnTx where n belongs to number of layers and Tx refers to the terminations like oxide (-O), fluoride (-F), hydroxide (-OH), and carboxylic (-COOH) etc. [44] The termination groups are usually attached to the MXene sheets during the etching, exfoliation and washing steps and their type usually depends on these procedures. As it is a newly discovered class of 2D materials several MAX phases haven been developed, etched and modified for various applications to see and improved the MXenes performances according to the requirement [45]. Tantalum carbide (Ta2CTx and Ta4C3Tx) is among one of the MXenes and is implemented for different scientific applications such as Ta2CTx has been investigated for nitro compound reduction [46], polymer membrane water electrolyser [47], as electrocatalyst with Iridium modifications [48] as well as OER catalyst [48]. Tantalum carbide MXene exhibited higher oxygen evolution reaction performances, considering the intrinsic property of the tantalum transition atom and higher oxygen adsorption. [48,49]. The presence of tantalum helps in building oxidation resistance and enables the catalyst to be less prone to oxidation [50]. Similarly, with the doping and functionalization tantalum carbide MXene have shown enhanced HER reactions also as bifunctional electrocatalyst which is due to their enhanced catalytic sites, surface area and density of states [46,51,52]. Ta4C3Tx MXene has been a major center of attraction of various scientific fields due to its unique mechanical, sensing and thermal characteristics. In medical field Ta4C3Tx has shown good conductivity as well as excellent immunomodulatory properties in organ transplantation [53,54]. Due to its exceptional mechanical strength Ta4C3Tx is also reported for enhanced shielding effects [55]. Ta4C3Tx is also used in fiber laser due to better absorption properties [56]. Ta4C3Tx has surpassed the conventional Ti-based MXenes for ammonia detection in the field of sensing [57]. Modified Ta4C3Tx showed potential in the field of photonic devices with increased performance [58]. Promising performances have been observed in the field of thiram detection and energy storge as supercapacitors [56,57]. In addition, Ta4C3Tx MXene exhibited excellent activities under thermal treatment and proved to be good thermal barriers [59,60]. Graphene tends to provide good stability to tantalum and an investigation showed that graphene/Ta4C3Tx performance is stable even under thermal treatments in the case of photo/electro aerogels [61,62]. Owing to the unique properties of tantalum carbide MXenes, excellent performances of Ta4C3Tx in other scientific fields, and better sensing and thermal properties in comparison to conventional MXenes, there is a need to explore Ta4C3Tx in the field of water splitting also. The study on Ta4C3Tx MXene and its surface engineering in the field will provide new insights to innovative catalytic designs of unconventional MXenes as well as Ta4C3Tx ability to act as an excellent thermal barrier would help in creating more sustainable and stable electrocatalysts under harsh environments.

Hence in this report we have synthesized Ta4C3Tx MXene using conventional hydrofluoric acid (HF) etching and polymer exfoliation routes. In addition, we also prepared nitrogen doped GQD via hydrothermal method. Next, we combined them both to generate GQD/Ta4C3Tx heterogeneous catalysts for water splitting application. We performed X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet (UV–vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopy to evaluate the catalysts synthesis and quality. The electrocatalytic activity was performed in alkaline media with a controlled engineering of Ta4C3Tx MXene sheets with nitrogen-doped GQD with a ratio of 1.5%–10%. The catalytic performances demonstrated enhanced HER and OER activities for GQD/Ta4C3Tx in comparison to bare GQD and MXene. Therefore, GQD/Ta4C3Tx proved to be efficient bifunctional heterogenous surfaces for overall water splitting.

Initially, the 48% HF (15 ml) solution was added in a Teflon beaker and transferred to continuous stirring with a slow addition of the MAX Ta4AlC3 phase (1g) to avoid its localized heating. Then the temperature was slowly increased up to 45 °C. The mixture was kept on the same temperature for two days and then was centrifuged and washed using deionized (DI) water to neutralize its pH. At the end the etched MXene (800 mg) was vacuum filtered and dried. The etched MXene powder was then added into DI water in a glass beaker under sonication at room temperature. Polymer TMAOH (6 ml) was added dropwise in the MXene (800 mg) solution under sonication and was kept for 40 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a hot plate for continuous stirring of 16 h. The exfoliated MXene powder was washed and dried in the oven to obtain solid form of Ta4C3Tx (740 mg). A synthesis schematic is also presented in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: A conventional method depicted in Fig. 1b was used to prepare nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots using citric acid and urea powders. An amount of 1.6-g citric acid (C6H8O7) and 1.4-g urea (CO(NH2)2) was added into 50 ml of DI water under stirring and the mixture was transferred to autoclave for hydrothermal process at 180 °C for 6 h. A pale-yellow graphene quantum dot solution was obtained at the end. A 10 wt% final yield of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) was obtained.

MXene Ta4C3Tx (1 mg/ml) was added into DI water (25 ml) in a glass beaker and sonication was performed for 10 min. After that graphene quantum dot solution (0.05 mg/ml in 25 ml DI water for 5% concentration) was added dropwise in the same solution and held under sonication for further 30 min. After that the solution was kept under continuous stirring at 80 °C for 2 h as shown in Fig. 1c. Finally, after cooling down the mixture, sediments were washed, vacuum filtered and dried under 60 °C overnight to obtain GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures. Several heterostructures were synthesized by controlling the amount of GQD as 1.5%, 3%, 5%, 7% and 10%. All these heterostructures were named as 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene as per the changing concentration of quantum dots.

Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffraction (XRD) tool was used for crystal structure analysis. scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed using Nova NanoSEM 450 to observe morphology and for elemental analysis. Talos F200X tool was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis to check morphology and crystal structure at small scale. In addition, ultraviolet (UV–vis) spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) were tested to analyze quantum dots. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using Thermo Scientific K-alpha model for structural chemical bonding. Also, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopy helped in analyzing in-plane and out of plane vibrational modes of the synthesized nano-heterostructures.

For water splitting application, CH instruments potentiostat system was used to check the catalytic performances of all prepared catalysts. A three-electrode system was used including nickel foam to coat catalysts slurry as working electrode, platinum plate as auxiliary/counter electrode and Hg/HgO as reference electrode. A basic electrolyte was prepared using 1 M (M) potassium hydroxide (KOH) for testing. For catalysts slurry preparation, 1 mg powder catalyst was mixed in DI water, ethanol and Nafion in a ratio of 5:5:1 to sonicate for 15 min. The slurry was coated on nickel foam and dried for 30 min at moderate temperature. The voltage window for HER activities was kept 0 to −1.4 V and for OER 0 to +1.4 V. EIS measurements were performed with in a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 1 × 105 Hz.

After the synthesis of GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano heterostructures, the crystal analysis of all phases is presented in Fig. 2 a and b. XRD results of etched Ta4C3Tx all the crystallographic planes of MXene including (002), (004), (101), (104), (018), (110) and (112). The (002) maxima appeared around 5.14° with a d-spacing of 17 Å consistent with the other published reports [47,56,58,63]. In GQD/Ta4C3Tx XRD pattern the intensity of (002) and (004) planes are increased with a d spacing of 17.6 Å. The increase in intensity corresponds to the change in surface termination and structure with the attachment of GQD. The GQD incorporation changes the interlayer spacing and provides a more ordered arrangement of 2D MXene planes in GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures [64,65]. There is a broadening of peaks with the appearance of new planes named as (006), (008) and (0010) depicting the dominance of 2D hkl MXene planes with reduce crystallite size after exfoliation [63]. In graphene dots XRD pattern the main hkl hump of (002) plane is clearly observed and represented their smooth small dimensionality and successful synthesis. The maxima of the peak is around 23.8° with a d-spacing of 3.7 nm. The (002) plane in quantum dots belongs to the CC sp2 hybridization in graphene quantum dots [13]. This (002) plane of GQDs is also present inside the GQD/Ta4C3Tx heterostructures XRD pattern and supports the successful synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and MXene nano heterostructures. The UV analysis of nitrogen-doped GQD in Fig. 2c showed an absorption peak at 332 nm which appeared due to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transition of CC and C–O bonds existing in the quantum dots structure [66]. In addition, the PL analysis in Fig. 2d depicted that with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the GQD showed photoluminescence in a range 400–600 nm representing the blue emission of the quantum dots. In PL spectra of GQD/Ta4C3Tx, there is a slight positive shift with reduced peak intensity due to the incorporation of GQD in MXene. The UV absorption and PL results are consistent with the already published work of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [67,68].

The morphology of quantum dots and Ta4C3Tx MXene sheets is shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM image of nitrogen doped quantum dots is taken by spin coating and drying a thin layer of GQD solution onto silicon wafer. Quantum dots of various sizes are visible in Fig. 3a which is due to clustering of GQD while preparing a thin film. Fig. 3b verified the layered morphology of Ta4C3Tx as well as comparatively more dense larger sheet size in comparison to conventional titanium carbide MXene. The sheets are more open and disordered in GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures after graphene quantum dots intercalation as visible in Fig. 3c. The elemental mapping and EDX results presented in Fig. 3d–g confirmed the presence of Ta and C along with O terminations as well as traces of Al and F inside the MXene structure.

In addition to analyze more at a smaller scale TEM analysis was also performed and the TEM image of Ta4C3Tx MXene sheets is shown in Fig. 4 a. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis of GQD/Ta4C3Tx depicted the hexagonal crystal symmetry in Fig. 4b elaborated the crystallinity retention of parent MAX phase as well as polycrystalline nature of GQD/Ta4C3Tx heterostructures while in Fig. 4c graphene quantum dots distributed over the MXene sheets are visible at a scale of 50 nm. The dotted red circles correspond to the adsorption/distribution and attachment of GQD with MXene sheets. Further a zoom in image at a scale of 5 nm is shown in Fig. 4d with yellow dotted lines depicting the atomic layers of MXene as well as red circles with straight yellow lines representing GQD atomic layers. The measured size of GQDs is around 2–2.5 nm. The SEM and TEM analysis are consistent with already reported studies [58,64].

The curves of FTIR analysis are presented in Fig. 5 a. There are CC sp2 vibrational bands along with C–H, C–N, C–O and C–OH. All these transitions come from the common stretching of the nitrogen doped GQD. Hence, these bands confirm the synthesis of the proposed GQD structure and its functional groups [69]. In MXene metal-carbon (Ta–C) and carbon-oxygen (C–O) transitions are there with a wider –OH shoulder around 3400 cm−1. The OH shoulder is shifted and decreased in GQD/MXene while there is a positive shift in metal-carbon vibrational mode (Ta–C) along with the clear vibrational changes in the carbon region (showed by dotted red circle in Fig. 5a) may attribute the bonding of GQD via Tx and exposed metallic atoms. A new peak appearance around 2300 cm−1 may correspond to the nitrile group and results due to bonding in between N–C of GQD/Ta4C3Tx [70]. The mixed stretching modes, peak shifts, and new peak appearances in the FTIR of GQD/Ta4C3Tx represent the successful synthesis of GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures [64,71]. Also, the Raman analysis peaks are shown in Fig. 5b. The vibrational modes present in GQD at around 1400 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 referred to as D and G bands of graphene. The D and G bands corresponds to defects as well as conventional graphitic vibrations in graphene [22]. While in Ta4C3Tx MXene the peaks at around 183.7 cm−1, 248.9 cm−1 and 650 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration modes ω3,4,5, ω6,7, and ω8,9,10 due to the presence of metal Ta, carbon and terminations Tx [72]. A shift and intensity change of these modes can be observed due to the removal of Al in etching as well as sheet exfoliation and variation in termination attachment due to bonding with other materials [73]. In Raman spectra of GQD/Ta4C3Tx distinct changes can be seen in flake, terminations and carbon regions. A shift and intensity increase in Ta–C Raman mode, appearance of D and G bands is due to the attachment of GQD with MXene [64]. Not only the intensity of D and G bands are changes but they also back shifted (D band at 1390 cm−1 and G band at 1581 cm−1) in the Raman spectra of GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures. The back shifting and intensity change of the D and G bands is due to the vibrational variations and stretching in Raman modes due to bonding of GQD with MXene [64,74].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to explore the chemical nature and composition of the Ta4C3Tx and GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures. Fig. S1a provides a full xps scan for Ta4C3Tx representing the corresponding tantalum (Ta), carbon (C), oxygen (O), and a very small peak of aluminum (Al2p). Aluminum is approximately removed from the MXene structure after the HF etching and exfoliation of sheets. Deconvolution of C1s and O1s spectral peaks (Figs. S1b–c) exhibited all the conventional peaks of metal-carbon, carbon-oxygen, carbon-carbon, hydroxyl groups along with a smaller peak of metal-oxygen in the tantalum carbide MXene [54,57]. All the distinct peaks of Ta, C, and O, are also present in GQD/Ta4C3Tx (Fig. 6 a) with the appearance of a new peak of nitrogen (N) around 400 eV [66]. The nitrogen peak corresponds to the distribution/attachment of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots over the MXene sheets [75]. The deconvolution of the Ta4f, C1s and O1s spectral peaks is also presented in Fig. 6b-d. In Ta4f metal-metal (Ta–Ta), metal-carbon (Ta–C) and metal-oxygen (Ta–O) compositional peaks are visible from 20 eV to 32 eV. In C1s there is a slight increase in peak intensity and a positive shift in C–C peak towards 284 eV corresponds to the bonding of sp2 C of GQD with MXene [60,64,71]. In addition, C-Tx (C–O and C–OH) peaks arise in between 285 eV–290 eV experienced a change in intensity and shifted forward which may be due to the nitrogen attachment of GQD with the MXene. Furthermore, deconvolution spectra of O1s at 526 eV–535 eV experienced an overall increase in oxygen intensity as well as a slight shift in the O-Tx peak corresponding to the bonding in between the oxygen groups of GQD and MXene in GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructure [64,76].

To evaluate the electrocatalytic performances GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructure were synthesized with varying amount of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) to see how the catalytic performances are affected with the controlled surface engineering of Ta4C3Tx MXene sheets via GQD. All the 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures with a varying amount of GQD (1.5%, 3%, 5%, 7% and 10%) in Ta4C3Tx were subjected to water splitting application in alkaline media and the OER polarization curves of these heterostructures with nickel foam (Ni foam), Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) and bare MXene (Ta4C3Tx) are shown in Fig. 7 a. OER activities are the oxygen evolution reactions occurring at the anode within the electrochemical cell. Ni foam showed a very low OER activity in comparison to bare Ta4C3Tx and GQD while all the heterostructures showed enhanced OER activities than Ta4C3Tx. In addition, the heterostructures 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene depicted improved OER performances than both GQD and Ta4C3Tx. These enhanced performances are due to increased active sites as well as high charge transfer in between electrolyte and electrocatalysts due to the GQD presence inside heterostructures [42,77]. The best OER activity was exhibited by 5%GQD/MXene which is probably the best compatible distribution of both GQD and MXene inside the GQD/Ta4C3Tx. After that the OER activities were a little reduced which perhaps due to overloading or agglomeration of GQDs in the 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene heterostructures [31]. The Tafel slopes at 10 mAcm−2 and 30 mAcm−2 were calculated to see the reaction kinetics for all the heterostructures and given in Table S1. These Tafel slopes at 10 mA/cm2 have also been plotted in Fig. 7b. The Tafel slopes for MXene Ta4C3Tx and GQD were 181 mVdec−1 and 128 mVdec−1 respectively and are much lower than the Ni foam. For 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures the calculated Tafel slopes were 112 mVdec−1, 102 mVdec−1, 95 mVdec−1, 141 mVdec−1, and 114 mVdec−1 corresponds to the two step Volmer-Heyrovsky process following faster reaction kinetics [42]. The over potentials (ηOER) for all the electrocatalysts to overcome a current density of 10 mAcm−2, 30 mAcm−2, and 50 mAcm−2 are shown in Fig. 7c. In comparison to achieving a current density of 10 mAcm−2, Ni foam achieved an overpotential of 493 mV while GQD and MXene showed an overpotential of 286 mV and 367 mV. For 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene, the obtained over potentials (ηOER) were 225 mV, 198 mV, 190 mV, 311 mV and 325 mV to breach the kinetic threshold for 10 mAcm2. For higher current densities, the Tafel slopes and overpotential both have been increased to overcome the complex reaction kinetics.

Hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) activities were also tested in 1 M KOH for all the electrocatalysts, and the polarization curves of current density and V vs RHE are displayed in Fig. 8 a. These reactions define the H2 production at cathode of an electrochemical cell. Ni foam HER performance is lower than both GQD and MXene while Ta4C3Tx MXene HER activity is better than the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots. Further, the HER activities of all the GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures are greatly improved than bare GQD and Ta4C3Tx. To evaluate the electrocatalytic reaction processes Tafel slopes were also calculated (@10 mAcm−2 and @30 mAcm−2) and shown in Fig. 8b and Table S1. The Tafel slopes for Ni foam, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, and Ta4C3Tx MXene were 267 mVdec−1, 189 mVdec−1, and 183 mVdec−1. For 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene, the obtained Tafel slopes were 140 mVdec−1, 134 mVdec−1, 116 mVdec−1, 145 mVdec−1, and 157 mVdec−1. Hence all the GQD/Ta4C3Tx nano-heterostructures have lower Tafel slopes in comparison to bare catalysts and faster reaction kinetics than them. [42]. The overpotentials (ηHER) for all the electrocatalysts to reach the current densities of 10 mAcm−2, 20 mAcm−2, and 30 mAcm−2 are exhibited in Fig. 8c. For overcoming a current density of 10 mAcm−2 ηHER for Ni foam, GQD, and Ta4C3Tx were 329 mV, 290 mV, and 216 mV. For the nano-heterostructures 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene, the overpotentials achieved for 10 mAcm−2 were 168 mV, 114 mV, 99 mV, 143 mV and 207 mV. The low overpotential could be due to more exposed active sites, which leads to more H2 adsorption with less potential needed to break the water molecules. Therefore, owing to their low overpotential compared to GQD and MXene alone, the GQD/MXene heterostructures are proved to be efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts.

To measure the resistance characteristics of electrocatalysts Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed by applying an AC bias at different frequencies. The Nyquist plots of the AC impedances of all the electrocatalysts are given in Fig. 9 a. These impedance measurements are important to analyze the electrolyte as well as electrode resistances to further evaluate the reaction transfer kinetics [42]. The impedance plots between imaginary impedance (Z″) and real impedance values (Z’) demonstrated that the overall internal resistance of the GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures are less in contrast to bare nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and Ta4C3Tx MXene. The obtained solution resistance (Rs) values for MXene, GQD, 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene are 1.65 Ω, 1.62 Ω, 1.7 Ω, 1.54 Ω, 1.05 Ω, 1.9 Ω, and 2.2 respectively. While the measured charge transfer resistances (Rct) for MXene, GQD, 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene were 1.3 × 103 Ω, 900 Ω, 150 Ω, 100 Ω, 80 Ω, 300 Ω, 740 Ω. The reduced charge transfer resistances of the GQD/MXene heterostructures corresponds to the improved conductivity as well as electrocatalytic reactions on the electrode surfaces [78]. Hence the EIS findings are in support of OER and HER catalytic measurements data. As referenced to enhanced HER and OER activities of synthesized GQD/Ta4C3Tx MXene nano-heterostructures are proved to be efficient bifunctional catalysts for overall water splitting. Fig. 9b shows full cell performance to assess the electrocatalysts' overall water splitting. These full cell performances demonstrate a two-electrode cell subjected to check the long-term viability of these difunctional electrocatalysts. Ta4C3Tx MXene itself needed a working cell voltage of 1.82 V to reach a current density of 10 mAcm−2. While after combining with the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, these working voltages are greatly reduced up to 1.5 V for 5%GQD/MXene \\ 5%GQD/MXene cell combination attributed towards the high structure compatibility and faster reaction mechanism. Among all the GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures the 5%GQD/MXene is proved to be the most efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting applications. The cell stability performance for HER and OER were also measured for a time duration of 20 h and the current density (mAcm−2) vs time outputs are presented in Fig. 9c. For OER activities bare Ta4C3Tx MXene exhibited performance stability up to 85% while GQD/MXene nanostructure was stable up to 97% corresponds towards the high structural durability even after a longer period of time. Similarly, for HER 86.6% of Ta4C3Tx MXene efficiency was maintained while for GQD/MXene heterostructure 90.4% of efficiency was preserved after 20 h. Therefore, GQD/Ta4C3Tx MXene nano-heterostructures have better potential as electrocatalysts for overall water splitting due to their superior HER and OER catalytic performances with good performance retention over extended periods of time.
GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures.

The enhanced catalytic mechanism in water splitting involves the efficient working of electrocatalyst interface in both HER and OER. Typically, in HER an efficient proton conversion into hydrogen gas occurs such as H2O + e− + X→ XHads + OH− and H2O + e− + XHads→ H2 + OH− + X. Where X and XHads correspond to the catalyst active cites and H adsorption on the X. While OER starts with the O–H bond breaking of adsorbed H2O molecules which leads to the production of O-containing species such as OOHX/OHX. The intermediates then oxidize and release O2 along with the active cites regeneration. The careful surface engineering of Ta4C3Tx sheets via GQDs enables GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures to provide higher HER and OER activities as bifunctional catalysts. Where Ta4C3Tx nanosheets play a role in providing a larger surface area, limited conduction, oxygen adsorption facility due to Ta transition atom [45,46,58,70]. While nitrogen doping in GQD enables more energy levels to regulate the active cites [39,40]. In addition, GQD aid to avoid restacking of Ta4C3Tx sheets, provide enough conductivity, additional surface area, abundant active sites for a uniform and fast charge transfer/ion transportation over the sheet surfaces to enhance the electrochemical reactions [39,41,42,64]. Also, their combination as graphene with tantalum together showed good stability for both HER and OER [62]. Hence they both in combination are proved to be a good bifunctional catalyst to design new and innovative electrocatalysts to solve the challenges in the field of water splitting.

In conclusion, Ta4C3Tx MXene was synthesized via HF etching and polymer exfoliation. In parallel, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) were also synthesized via simple hydrothermal route. A cost effective and easy solvothermal approach was utilized to prepare GQD/MXene nano-heterostructures. Through carefully regulated surface engineering of MXene sheets, a variety of heterostructures were created with varying concentrations of quantum dots, ranging from 1.5% to 10%. XRD, UV–vis, PL, Raman, FTIR, SEM, TEM and XPS helped in evaluating the crystallinity, chemical structure, elemental composition and morphology of the prepared nano-heterostructures. Further these 1.5%GQD/MXene, 3%GQD/MXene, 5%GQD/MXene, 7%GQD/MXene and 10%GQD/MXene heterostructures were implemented as electrocatalysts to test the OER and HER activities in alkaline electrolyte. They exhibited enhanced catalytic activities than bare Ta4C3Tx MXene and GQD. Among all the nano-heterostrutures 5%GQD/MXene presented best HER, OER performances due to fast reaction kinetics and abundant active sites. Additionally, its lowest charge transfer resistance from EIS analysis, highest stability over an extended time and lowest two cell electrode working voltage (1.5 V) enables it to have a greater potential for designing advanced catalysts.
